content.text: Tool Granularity

Granularity determines how much work each tool does in a single call. A tool that does too little forces the model to chain many calls for basic tasks. A tool that does too much removes the model's ability to reason about intermediate steps. The right granularity gives the model complete information per call while keeping each tool's scope narrow enough to be predictable and safe.

attrs.alt: Tool granularity spectrum from too fine to too coarse
attrs.title: Tool Granularity Spectrum

content.text: A useful heuristic: one tool = one verb + one noun.
get_user
send_email
search_products
. Avoid
manage_user
handle_communication
, or
do_everything
Too Fine: The Micro-Tool Problem

When each tool does a single atomic step, the model burns through turns just assembling the data it needs. Every tool call is an API round-trip that adds latency and token cost. Worse, the model can make mistakes in intermediate steps that cascade into errors in later calls.
Too Coarse: The God-Tool Problem

A tool that accepts free-form instructions and does anything removes the model's ability to plan. The model cannot predict side effects, cannot selectively invoke sub-capabilities, and you cannot audit what happened. God-tools also produce schemas with too many optional parameters, which confuses the model.
The Merge-vs-Split Decision
The key question when deciding to merge or split tools is:
does the model need to make a decision between these steps?
If yes, keep them separate. If the steps always run together with no branching, merge them.
Using Optional Parameters vs Multiple Tools
Common variations of the same action belong in one tool as optional parameters, not as separate tools. Separate tools should have meaningfully different effects — not just different filters.
Measuring Granularity in Practice

A quick diagnostic: run your agent on 20 representative prompts and count how many tool calls it takes per task. If average calls per task is high but tasks are simple, your tools are too fine. If the model frequently calls the same tool with unrelated parameters, it may be too coarse.
Granularity and Safety

Granularity affects safety, not just efficiency. Fine-grained read tools expose less surface area for mistakes. Write operations especially benefit from being split from reads — it forces the model to reason explicitly before making changes and lets you add confirmation steps only on the write path.
Summary
Each tool should do one thing: one verb + one noun. Too-fine tools waste turns; too-coarse tools remove model control.
Return everything the model needs for its next decision in a single call — avoid forcing sequential lookups for related data.
Merge tools when they are always called in sequence with no decision between them. Split when there is a meaningful branching point.
Prefer optional parameters over multiple tools for common variations of the same action.
Always separate read tools from write tools — different risk profiles, different failure modes, different audit needs.
Measure granularity by counting average tool calls per task across representative prompts; 2–3 calls per task is usually healthy.
